title: Move.toml pins pas/ptb to branch `main` — non-reproducible builds
description: Both `pas` and `ptb` git dependencies are declared with `rev = "main"` (a branch name) rather than a commit SHA. Although `Move.lock` currently pins to `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`, a subsequent `sui move build --lock` or a fresh clone that regenerates the lockfile will rotate to whatever `main` points to at that moment. This silently changes the security surface of the Hadron package — the PAS `policy`, `request`, `send_funds`, `clawback_funds`, `templates`, and `internal::Permit` types all come from that dep.
impact: Non-reproducible builds. Upstream PAS changes (e.g., altered approval semantics, added required approvals, changed `resolve_balance` destination) would be picked up silently on the next lock regeneration. This is also a supply-chain risk — anyone with commit access to `MystenLabs/pas:main` effectively has code-execution over Hadron's next build.
recommendation: Change `rev = "main"` to `rev = "b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc"` (or a later audited tag) in both `pas` and `ptb` entries. Add a CI check that `Move.toml` does not reference a branch. Consider tagging a PAS release in `MystenLabs/pas` and pinning to the tag.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: No Move tests exist for any new PAS module
description: There is no `tests/` directory in the Hadron package and no `#[test]` or `#[test_only]` function in `pas_admin.move`, `pas_supply.move`, or `pas_transfer.move`. All new flows (register_pas_asset, add/remove/set whitelist, mint, burn, seize, approve_transfer) are completely untested. The only test-only helpers are `treasury::create_for_testing` / `share_for_testing`, which are phase-1 code.
impact: High-stakes compliance logic (whitelist gating, seize path, clawback-based burn, PAS policy integration) has no regression coverage. Any upstream PAS change or Hadron refactor can silently break the authorization surface. Reviewers (and CI) have no guard against regressions in critical invariants such as `!is_pas_coin` guards in `supply::mint/redeem`, whitelist send/receive independence, or request-approval ordering.
recommendation: Add Move unit tests that cover at minimum: (a) `supply::mint`/`redeem` aborts with `EPasCoinNotAllowed` on PAS-registered T; (b) `pas_supply::mint` requires recipient whitelisted with `can_receive=true`; (c) `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` rejects non-whitelisted sender and non-whitelisted recipient separately; (d) `pas_supply::seize` does NOT require source whitelisted but DOES require destination whitelisted; (e) `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` rejects non-empty `TreasuryCap`; (f) `set_whitelist_send/receive` after `remove_from_whitelist` aborts with `ENotInWhitelist`.

title: Public query functions abort on non-PAS coin types instead of returning false
description: `is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, and `can_receive<T>` unconditionally call `dynamic_object_field::borrow` on `keys::whitelist_key<T>()`. For any coin type T that is not a PAS asset (i.e., the whitelist DOF was never added by `add_pas_coin_caps`), this aborts inside the framework with an opaque `EFieldDoesNotExist` error instead of returning `false`. Callers (including off-chain indexers) expecting `is_whitelisted<T>` to be a safe predicate will crash.
impact: Opaque aborts in read-only queries. Off-chain tooling and frontend dApps that probe whitelist state across arbitrary coin types cannot distinguish 'coin is not PAS' from 'coin is PAS but addr not whitelisted'. Forces every caller to first call `is_pas_coin<T>()` as a guard.
recommendation: Guard each query with `is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)` and return `false` when the DOF is absent. Example: `public fun is_whitelisted<T>(treasury: &Treasury, addr: address): bool { if (!is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)) return false; let wl: &Table<address, WhitelistEntry> = dynamic_object_field::borrow(&treasury.id, keys::whitelist_key<T>()); wl.contains(addr) }`. Do the same for `can_send` and `can_receive`.

title: pas_supply::mint/burn/seize do not guard against non-PAS coin T
description: `pas_supply::mint<T>`, `::burn<T>`, and `::seize<T>` rely on `&Auth<_, T>` as the caller-side gate, but `auth::new_auth` only asserts `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()` — true for BOTH regulated coins (registered via `admin::register_coin`) and PAS coins. A permission-holder for MintPermission/RedeemPermission/SeizePermission on a regulated (non-PAS) coin can call `pas_supply::mint<T>` and will only abort deep inside `assert_can_receive<T>` (dynamic_object_field::borrow) because the whitelist DOF does not exist. `burn` and `seize` additionally require a `Policy<Balance<T>>` which is only constructible for PAS-registered T, so the non-PAS path for those two aborts at Request construction time — but the error surface is opaque.
impact: Opaque abort rather than a clean error message. A legitimate admin accidentally targeting the wrong flow (regulated vs PAS) has no precise diagnostic. Not exploitable (no balance or authorization bypass), but clutters monitoring/debug and mirrors the guard added in `supply::mint/redeem` asymmetrically.
recommendation: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin)` at the top of `pas_supply::mint`, `burn`, and `seize`. Mirrors the `EPasCoinNotAllowed` guard in `supply::mint/redeem` and makes the error path symmetric.

title: pas_supply::seize gated on mint/redeem pause — compliance cannot seize while paused
description: `seize` calls `treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()`, meaning a compliance team cannot seize funds while mint/redeem is paused. The standard operational intent is the opposite: pausing mint/redeem during an incident SHOULD NOT lock out the compliance team's seize capability — if anything, pausing is often accompanied by seizure to contain damage. The `SeizePermission` is separate from MintPermission/RedeemPermission for exactly this reason, but gating seize on the mint/redeem pause flag collapses the two concerns.
impact: During an incident response, pausing mint/redeem also freezes seize. The incident response playbook has to unpause → seize → repause, which is both a race-condition window (an attacker can move funds in the unpause window) and an accounting mess.
recommendation: Remove `treasury.assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()` from `seize`. Seize should be governed solely by the `SeizePermission` role and the PAS Policy's clawback approval — not by the app-layer mint/redeem pause. If a distinct pause-for-seize control is desired, introduce a separate `SeizePausedKey<T>` rather than reusing mint/redeem pause.

title: pas_transfer::approve_transfer conflates mint/redeem pause with transfer pause
description: `approve_transfer` checks `!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()` to gate transfer approvals. This is a design choice: pausing mint/redeem also pauses every PAS transfer between whitelisted accounts. The Notion spec (§7.2) says transfers should be gated on 'not paused' — but it does not specify whether that is the mint/redeem pause or a distinct transfer pause. Treating them as the same pause removes operational flexibility (e.g., pause issuance while allowing existing holders to continue transacting among whitelisted accounts).
impact: Institutions cannot pause issuance (mint/redeem) without also halting secondary transfers. A common regulatory ask is the inverse: freeze transfers while preserving issuance/redemption with KYC counterparties. Current design does not allow that granularity.
recommendation: Introduce a separate `TransferPausedKey<T>` DF and `pause_transfer` / `unpause_transfer` flows (with their own permission types), and gate `approve_transfer` on the new pause flag. Keep the mint/redeem pause orthogonal.

title: update_transfer_template emits no event — silent state change
description: `update_transfer_template<T>` changes the Templates registry entry (via `pas::templates::set_template_command`), which alters how the SDK autoresolves a transfer — but Hadron emits no event on the update. `register_pas_asset` emits `PasAssetRegistered<T>` indirectly via `add_pas_coin_caps`, but there is no `TransferTemplateUpdated<T>` emission. Off-chain tooling and auditors must cross-reference the upstream Templates DOF state to detect a template change.
impact: Observability gap. A malicious or buggy upgrade path that re-registers the template pointing to a different package (e.g., via package upgrade shuffling) is undetectable by Hadron event consumers.
recommendation: Add a `TransferTemplateUpdated<phantom T>` event with the package address and approval type, and emit it from both `register_pas_asset` (through the internal helper) and `update_transfer_template`.

title: register_transfer_template uses `with_defining_ids` — update after upgrade cannot repoint
description: `register_transfer_template` keys the package address for the template using `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>().address_string()`. Per the Move stdlib contract (sui-framework move-stdlib `type_name.move` L42-45), `with_defining_ids` returns the 'defining ID (the ID of the package in storage that first introduced the type)'. That is the v1 original publication of Hadron. When Hadron is upgraded to v2, the TransferApproval type still has the v1 defining ID, so calling `update_transfer_template` in v2 writes the SAME address back — giving the appearance of an update without any observable change. The operation is effectively a no-op after upgrade.
impact: If the team later adds new logic to `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` (e.g., a KYC registry check, rate limiting, new validation) in a package upgrade, the SDK-side template will keep pointing at the v1 package whose logic is unchanged. Sui's PTB executor resolves the package to any on-chain version with a matching function signature, so the NEW logic may still run depending on runtime resolution — but the design invariant 'update_transfer_template can fix a broken template after upgrade' is not satisfied by the current implementation.
recommendation: Key the template on the current (latest) package address, e.g. use `@hadron` resolved at compile time in the current upgrade, or accept an explicit `package_id: address` parameter so operators can pass the upgraded address. Document clearly the post-upgrade semantics. Confirm with the PAS team whether `set_template_command` against the same address is intentionally idempotent.

title: add_to_whitelist/remove_from_whitelist/set_whitelist_* do not assert is_pas_coin
description: The whitelist mutators gate on `&Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`, whose construction (auth::new_auth) only requires `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()`. A non-PAS coin that is also registered via `admin::register_coin` would allow a WhitelistManagePermission holder to obtain an Auth for T; the subsequent `treasury.add_to_whitelist<T>` aborts opaquely inside `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut` because the whitelist DOF does not exist for a non-PAS T.
impact: Poor error surface. A compliance officer operating on the wrong coin type gets an uninformative framework abort instead of `ENotPasCoin`.
recommendation: In `treasury::add_to_whitelist/remove_from_whitelist/set_whitelist_send/set_whitelist_receive`, add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin)` as the first line before the DOF borrow.

title: Spec drift: single WhitelistManagePermission collapses add/remove/set into one role
description: The 'PAS Modules' Notion spec defines distinct `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission` for `add_to_whitelist` / `remove_from_whitelist` / `set_whitelist_send` / `set_whitelist_receive`. The implementation collapses these into a single `WhitelistManagePermission` granting all four operations. This is a deliberate code/spec divergence documented in the context bundle (§7.2 'Spec drift note'), but it is a compliance-relevant decision worth explicit sign-off from the spec owner: a single role with 'add+remove+set' loses the separation-of-duties pattern where one team can whitelist but not de-whitelist (or vice versa).
impact: Reduced principle-of-least-privilege granularity. An operator with whitelist-add authority also has whitelist-remove and whitelist-modify, which may not be the intended RBAC split per the design spec.
recommendation: Either (a) document and sign off on the decision to collapse the permissions, or (b) split into `WhitelistAddPermission`, `WhitelistRemovePermission`, `WhitelistUpdatePermission` to align with the spec. If collapsed, rename the permission to match the spec's semantic intent (e.g., `WhitelistAdminPermission`).
evidence:
// ============ PAS Whitelist ============

public struct WhitelistManagePermission {}

title: register_pas_asset creates Policy + template before the treasury-side registration check
description: The happy path of `register_pas_asset` calls (in order): `policy::new_for_currency` (upstream abort-on-exists check in `pas/policy.move` L51), set required approvals, `register_transfer_template`, `policy::share`, then finally `treasury.add_pas_coin_caps` (which aborts on `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` if the Hadron treasury already has T registered via the regulated path). This means a second registration attempt against a coin already registered as regulated via `admin::register_coin` first creates and shares a Policy object for T in the PAS namespace (and writes a template), then aborts — rolling the whole tx back, so no state is persisted. This is semantically safe but the upstream-first ordering makes the error surface confusing when the conflict is actually in the Hadron treasury.
impact: Minor ergonomics. Error message from `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` happens only after the upstream PAS side has been exercised. If the upstream `new_for_currency` had weaker guarantees (e.g., did not rollback cleanly), this ordering could leak state.
recommendation: Insert an explicit early check `assert!(!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered)` at the start of `register_pas_asset`, before `policy::new_for_currency`. This makes the error path deterministic and short-circuits the upstream call.

title: Object-owned accounts: assert_can_receive uses synthetic address, needs documentation
description: `pas_supply::mint` calls `treasury.assert_can_receive<T>(account.owner())`. Per upstream `pas::account::new_auth_as_object` (pas/account.move L132-134), an Account that is owned by a UID-owned object uses `uid.to_inner().to_address()` as its owner — a synthetic address. For that case, the compliance team needs to whitelist the synthetic address, not the wallet that controls the parent object. This is undocumented in Hadron's module docs and will bite operators trying to mint into object-owned accounts.
impact: Operational confusion. A compliance officer trying to onboard an object-owned account (e.g., a DAO treasury modeled as a Sui object) must know to compute `object::id(parent).to_address()` and whitelist that synthetic address rather than any wallet — a non-obvious convention drawn from the upstream PAS SDK.
recommendation: Add a module-level doc comment to `pas_supply` and `pas_admin::add_to_whitelist` explaining that for object-owned PAS accounts the whitelistable address is `object::id(parent_obj).to_address()`. Reference the upstream account.move L132-134 behavior.

title: WhitelistEntry lacks timestamp/expiry — no regulatory audit trail
description: `WhitelistEntry` stores only `can_send: bool` and `can_receive: bool`. There is no timestamp (added_at, updated_at), no expiry, no reason code, and no associated off-chain identifier. For a compliance-regulated asset, audit trails typically need an added_at / last_modified_by / expiry field for KYC periodic-refresh cycles.
impact: No on-chain record of when an address was whitelisted or by whom (beyond the WhitelistAdded event which is an event, not queryable state). Compliance audits requiring 'show me the whitelist as of date X' must replay events rather than query state.
recommendation: Consider extending `WhitelistEntry` with `{ can_send: bool, can_receive: bool, added_at_ms: u64, expires_at_ms: Option<u64> }` and pass `&Clock` into `add_to_whitelist`. Alternative: rely entirely on the event stream and document the audit-trail model explicitly.

title: pas.ts mintPasTokens/sendBalance use JS number for u64 amount — silent precision loss above 2^53
description: `mintPasTokens(..., amount: number)` passes `amount: number` straight into the Move call generator. JavaScript numbers are IEEE-754 doubles; integer precision holds only up to 2^53-1 (~9.007e15). For a 6-decimal coin, that limits safe values to ~9 billion tokens. At 9-decimal precision (typical for SUI-like tokens) the safe ceiling drops to ~9 million tokens. The same issue applies to `full-setup.ts` line 114 (`amount: 100_000`) and any caller passing a u64-range value. Amounts above 2^53 will silently lose precision.
impact: Silent off-by-many error for large amounts. An institution minting `10_000_000_000_000_000` (1e16) into a PAS account will receive a different amount on-chain than expected. Bug is invisible in test flows using small amounts (1e6).
recommendation: Change the signature to `amount: bigint` and pass as `tx.pure.u64(amount)` (or the equivalent in the generated codegen). Apply the same fix to `full-setup.ts` where literals like `1_000_000` should become `1_000_000n`. Add a TypeScript lint rule forbidding `number` types for amount / balance parameters.

title: pkg() returns empty string when deploy-output.json missing — unhelpful downstream failure
description: `pkg()` reads `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1` and returns `''` if neither is populated (i.e., deploy-output.json is missing). Every PAS operation in `pas.ts` uses this via template strings like `${pkg()}::permissions::MintPermission`, producing invalid type strings like `::permissions::MintPermission`. The on-chain transaction will fail with an obscure MoveCompilation/type-parse error rather than a clean 'deployment artifact missing' error.
impact: Confusing UX when the script is run before a successful deploy. The actual error occurs only at signAndExecute, far from the root cause.
recommendation: Make `pkg()` throw synchronously if both v1 and latest are empty: `if (!CONSTANTS.package.v1 && !CONSTANTS.package.latest) throw new Error('No package ID configured — run deploy first or set CONSTANTS.package')`. Or change the fallback semantics to force callers to pass a packageId.

title: deployAndRegisterPasAsset uses substring matching on framework type names
description: Object ID extraction for TreasuryCap/MetadataCap/Currency uses `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`, `t.includes('::coin_registry::MetadataCap<')`, and `t.includes('::coin_registry::Currency<')`. Substring matching on fully-qualified type names is fragile: (a) the framework could rename a module in a future upgrade; (b) a user-defined type could accidentally contain the same substring; (c) the match does not validate the type argument inside the angle brackets matches the deployed coin type. A deploy yielding multiple caps (or a caps-bearing object with a surprising type) can silently pick the wrong object.
impact: Wrong IDs extracted from the deploy result under edge cases (multi-cap deploys, future framework renames). Subsequent `registerPasAsset` call silently passes the wrong cap object and aborts on-chain, or — worse — registers the wrong coin.
recommendation: Parse the type string with a proper regex that includes the exact coin type argument, e.g. `^0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<${coinType}>$` after constructing coinType earlier. Alternatively, use the `@mysten/sui` coin-registry helpers to query the exact cap for a given coin type. Add assertions that exactly one match is found.

title: deployPasTokenTemplate has hard-coded bytecode with no regeneration toolchain or validation
description: The bytecode is a long hex literal checked into source with no reproducible build process: no script or npm task to regenerate from `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move`, no hash/digest check at runtime, and no comment on the toolchain/sui version used to produce the bytes. Reviewers cannot verify the bytecode corresponds to the published `.move` source, and a future framework upgrade (e.g., coin_registry v2 signature change) that invalidates the bytes will silently produce an invalid-module publish error.
impact: Trust gap: the bytes could silently diverge from the .move source. A malicious PR could edit the hex blob to publish a different coin module than the source suggests. Operationally, drift between Sui versions will produce inscrutable publish failures.
recommendation: Add a `scripts/src/deployPasTokenTemplate/build-bytecode.ts` that compiles `token_template_pas/` with `sui move build`, extracts the single module, and writes the hex to `getBytecode.ts` with a SHA-256 digest comment. Wire it as a prebuild step. Add a runtime assertion that the bytecode is valid (e.g., via `get_constants(bytecode)` returning a non-empty set).

title: deployPasTokenTemplate passes 0x1 and 0x2 only — relies on implicit framework-dep resolution
description: `tx.publish` declares dependencies `[0x1, 0x2]` (MoveStdlib, Sui framework). `coin_registry` lives at `0x2`, so that is implicitly covered. However the upstream `sui::coin_registry` module also imports `sui::derived_object` and `sui::transfer` (both in 0x2) and the template code imports only `sui::coin` and `sui::coin_registry` — so 0x1+0x2 appears sufficient. But this inventory is fragile: if the token_template source later uses `sui::clock` (which is fine, still in 0x2) or any address-based stdlib, 0x1+0x2 still work; but if it adds `sui::zklogin_verified_id` or any new framework package, a new dep entry would be needed. There is no assertion that the declared deps cover the bytecode's actual imports.
impact: Brittle publish-time dependency list. A future edit to the token_template source that adds an import from a new framework package will fail to publish with an obscure 'Missing dependency' error. Also, the Sui framework `0x2` uses address-level ABI versioning that could change across runtime versions.
recommendation: Extract the dependency list from the bytecode itself (parse the `ModuleHandle` entries) and assert the set equals `{0x1, 0x2}`. Or use `@mysten/sui`'s higher-level publish helper that inspects the bytecode's declared imports.

title: full-setup.ts uses global PASClient with stale network binding
description: `const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` is constructed at module load time and reuses the same PASClient for all calls. This makes NETWORK effectively compile-time. Worse, the `as any` cast hides that `PASClient` may expect a stricter SuiClient interface than `getClient` returns — any API mismatch is silently coerced and will crash at runtime.
impact: (a) Scripts cannot easily switch network mid-run. (b) Any type-level contract drift between `@mysten/pas` and `@mysten/sui` versions is hidden by the `as any` cast and surfaces only at runtime.
recommendation: Remove the `as any` cast; if there is a type mismatch between `@mysten/pas` and `@mysten/sui`, upgrade one side or file an upstream issue. Construct `PASClient` lazily inside each function to pick up the current client.
evidence: const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });

title: PasTransferApproved event emitted even though the transfer is not yet resolved
description: `approve_transfer` emits `PasTransferApproved<T>` immediately after stamping the approval witness on the Request. But approval is a precondition — the actual movement of funds happens in `pas::send_funds::resolve_balance<C>` (upstream, packages/pas/sources/requests/send_funds.move L62-71), which the SDK calls in a later PTB command. If `resolve_balance` aborts (policy versioning check, required_approvals mismatch) the approve event is still emitted because events are not rolled back on same-transaction abort within the same PTB — wait, they actually ARE rolled back on tx abort. So this is only misleading if the SDK builds a PTB that approves but never resolves (partial transfer — technically impossible with the hot-potato Request).
impact: Minor observability polish. The event name `PasTransferApproved` suggests 'approved for transfer' not 'transfer successful'. A consumer seeing the event should understand it means 'approval stamped, resolution pending/imminent in the same tx'. Misnamed events cause dashboard confusion.
recommendation: Rename the event to `PasTransferApprovalStamped<T>` or `PasTransferApprovalGranted<T>` to reflect that it records the approval step, not the final transfer completion. Document in the event struct that resolution is handled by upstream PAS and emits no Hadron-side event.

title: pas_admin::register_pas_asset has unused `version` parameter in its own body
description: `register_pas_asset` takes `version: &Version` but never calls `version.check_is_valid()` directly — the version check is only performed when the caller constructs the `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>`. If that auth was constructed in an earlier PTB (unlikely for `TreasuryAuth` which has drop but not store, so lives within one PTB), the version-at-call-time may differ from version-at-auth-construction. This matters only if the version changes mid-tx, which cannot happen. But the parameter is only used via the nested `register_transfer_template` which also does not call check_is_valid. So effectively the parameter is documented surface without runtime effect in this function.
impact: Subtle invariant: the version check for this code path comes only from auth construction, not from the function itself. If any future refactor drops the auth-side version check, this function becomes unguarded. Also, `register_transfer_template` internal fn takes `version` but does not use it.
recommendation: Either (a) add an explicit `version.check_is_valid()` at the top of `register_pas_asset` and `update_transfer_template`, mirroring the pattern in `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`; or (b) drop the unused `version` parameter from the internal helper `register_transfer_template` and document at the call site that the version check is delegated to auth.

title: pas_transfer::approve_transfer is permissionless and unauthenticated
description: `approve_transfer` is `public fun` with no auth proof — anyone holding a `&mut Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>` can call it. This is intentional per the PAS autoresolve design: the caller already demonstrated ownership of the source funds (they were withdrawn from an Account via `send_balance`), and the approval logic is purely a compliance check. But the permissionless nature is worth an explicit doc note so reviewers don't later assume auth is missing. Additionally: since Hadron emits a `PasTransferApproved<T>` event, any observer can build an event-spam dashboard by repeatedly invoking approve_transfer against the same in-flight Request — duplicate `request.approve` will abort (`VecSet::insert` rejects duplicates, pas/request.move L26), so this is not exploitable.
impact: None directly; attempt to exploit aborts. But the permissionless nature should be explicitly documented for the audit.
recommendation: Expand the module-level doc comment to explicitly state: 'approve_transfer is permissionless by design — the compliance gate is the whitelist check, not caller identity. A request can only be approved once per type, so duplicate invocations abort in pas::request.' Link to the PAS docs section 'How clients use templates'.

title: WhitelistKey docstring says `Table<address, bool>` but actual type is Table<address, WhitelistEntry>
description: The docstring on `WhitelistKey<phantom T>` reads 'DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist (PAS assets).' — but the actual value stored (see treasury.move L259-261) is `Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` where WhitelistEntry holds two booleans (can_send, can_receive). The doc is stale from an earlier bool-only design.
impact: Developer confusion when reading the module. Non-functional, but misleading for future maintainers who will assume the DOF stores a flat boolean flag.
recommendation: Update the docstring to `/// DOF key for Table<address, WhitelistEntry> whitelist (PAS assets).` to reflect the actual value type.
evidence:
/// DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist (PAS assets).
public struct WhitelistKey<phantom T>() has copy, drop, store;

title: Duplicated EMintRedeemPaused string literal across supply.move, pas_transfer.move
description: `pas_transfer::EMintRedeemPaused` ("Mint and redeem operations are paused for this PAS asset") and `treasury::EMintRedeemDisabled` ("Mint and redeem operations are disabled for this coin") describe the same state with slightly different words. The pause-check in `approve_transfer` uses its own local `EMintRedeemPaused` constant rather than reusing `treasury::EMintRedeemDisabled` via `assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>()`. Code divergence: two error codes for the same semantic.
impact: Monitoring/alerting treats the two errors as distinct when they are the same condition. Minor maintenance burden.
recommendation: In `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`, replace the local `assert!(!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>(), EMintRedeemPaused);` with `treasury::assert_mint_redeem_enabled<T>(treasury);`. Delete the local `EMintRedeemPaused` constant.
evidence:
#[error]
const EMintRedeemPaused: vector<u8> = b"Mint and redeem operations are paused for this PAS asset";

title: full-setup.ts grants RegisterCoin via sender only — no separate register-coin role in demo
description: The demo creates only two roles — `minter` (mint, redeem) and `compliance_officer` (pauseMintRedeem, unpauseMintRedeem, denylistAdd, denylistRemove, whitelistManage). Neither role has `registerCoin` or `seize`. The `registerPasAsset` call later in the flow relies on the bootstrap admin (sender) having `ManageRolesPermission` — which includes `RegisterCoinPermission` via the role? Actually no: `bootstrap_admin` only gets `ManageRolesPermission` (phase-1 convention). So this demo, as written, will fail at the `registerPasAsset` step when the sender's bootstrap admin role cannot satisfy `RegisterCoinPermission`. Unless the phase-1 role setup implicitly grants registerCoin to the bootstrap admin (out of scope to verify here).
impact: The example may not run end-to-end without manual role assignment, making it a poor reference for integrators.
recommendation: Verify that the bootstrap admin has `RegisterCoinPermission`; if not, extend the demo's role list with an admin role carrying `['registerCoin', 'updateMetadata']`. Also add a `seizeOfficer` role so the full PAS surface can be demonstrated end-to-end.
